title: Adding Noise as Augmentation
concept: Noise injection deliberately adds random pixel noise to training images — the opposite goal of the denoising covered in Unit 94 — so the CNN learns features that survive real sensor noise at deployment.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Hue jitter rotates colors around the color wheel, and saturation jitter changes their vividness. What do both leave untouched?

options.text: The position and shape of everything in the frame
Nothing — hue and saturation jitter already change everything

exercises.explain: Right — color jitter never moves content. Now for a photometric trick that revisits an old friend: noise.

cards.title: Old TV static, on purpose this time
cards.body: Back in Unit 94, you learned that image noise is random, unwanted speckling that sneaks into photos from a camera sensor, especially in low light. Here's the twist: during training, we can add that exact same kind of static to images on purpose.

cards.title: What is noise injection?
cards.body: Noise injection deliberately adds random noise — like Gaussian noise — to training images. Instead of a network only ever seeing clean, pristine photos, it learns to find real patterns even when random speckle is layered on top.

cards.title: Like a vaccine, for pixels
cards.body: A vaccine exposes your immune system to a weakened, controlled version of a threat so it learns to handle the real thing later. Noise injection exposes a CNN to a controlled, artificial dose of noise during training so it's ready for the real sensor noise it will meet at deployment.

cards.title: Cheap camera, dim room, real deployment
cards.body: A security app trained only on crisp, well-lit studio photos can fall apart on a cheap phone camera's grainy low-light footage at 2am. Adding random Gaussian noise to a fraction of training images teaches the model to rely on real structure, not on pixel-perfect cleanliness.

cards.title: This isn't a contradiction of Unit 94
cards.body: It's easy to think: "Unit 94 was about removing noise, so adding it now must be a mistake." They're actually solving different problems. Denoising cleans up a specific image for viewing or downstream use. Noise injection during training is a stress test that makes the model itself sturdier — a sturdier model needs less denoising to work well later.

cards.title: Denoising vs. noise injection
cards.body: Same concept, opposite direction, different purpose:

left.text:
Removes noise from an image
Applied to clean up a specific photo
Goal: a clearer picture

right.text:
Adds noise during training only
Applied to training images on purpose
Goal: a sturdier model

exercises.prompt: What is the purpose of adding random noise to training images?

options.text: To teach the model to rely on real structure instead of pixel-perfect cleanliness, so it's robust to real sensor noise later
To permanently clean up the dataset's photos
To reposition objects within each photo
To reduce the total number of training images needed

exercises.explain: Right — noise injection is a controlled stress test that builds robustness to real-world sensor noise.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Noise injection during training and denoising from Unit 94 are trying to accomplish the same goal.
exercises.explain: False. Denoising cleans up an image for use; noise injection deliberately adds noise during training to make the model itself sturdier.

exercises.sentence: Noise injection adds random pixel noise on ___, unlike Unit 94's denoising, which removes it.
exercises.explain: Noise injection is intentional — it's added on purpose as a training stress test, not an accidental sensor artifact.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes noise injection as a training augmentation?

options.text: Randomly scattering small speckles of pixel-value variation across a training image before feeding it to the model
Running a median filter over an image to smooth it out
Cropping a training image to a smaller square

exercises.explain: Correct — deliberately scattering random speckle onto a training image is noise injection.

exercises.prompt: A wildlife-camera model trained only on crisp daytime photos fails badly on real grainy, low-light night footage from the field. A teammate suggests denoising every training image first to make them as clean as possible. What's the better photometric augmentation approach?

options.text: Instead, inject random noise into some training images so the model learns robust features that survive noise, matching the grainy conditions it will actually face
Denoise every training image, since a model trained on cleaner data always generalizes better to noisier real-world data
Only use geometric augmentation, since noise is unrelated to pixel values
Remove all low-light examples from the dataset entirely

exercises.explain: Exactly! Training on deliberately noisy images builds the robustness that only-clean training data can't. Next: stacking geometric and photometric augmentation together into one pipeline.
